title: Research Reveals That 60 Percent of Rebates Are Not Redeemed
author: santella.com / JPS Rebate & Fulfillment Articles (staff)
abstract: Rebate & fulfillment industry trade-press article on santella.com/JPS citing Aberdeen Group's Peter Kastner: about 60 percent of buyers who could redeem computer-related rebates do not try, and of the 40 percent who try, half experience problems. The piece contextualizes the stat with a process-level explanation of why rebate redemption fails: three-party (manufacturer/retailer/fulfillment-house) coordination, 15-90 day sponsor-to-fulfillment-house payment cycles, check-clearance waits, and unpaid invoices that fulfillment houses have reported to the FTC. This is a distinct publication from the Gary Gray (Chatham Journal) and Lorrie Grant (USA Today) rebate articles in the same collection — it pairs Kastner's stat with rebate-industry supply-side process detail.
importance_rationale: Frames Kastner's 60% non-redemption stat from the rebate-fulfillment industry perspective, linking consumer behavior to the operational mechanics of fulfillment houses — a dimension missing from consumer-press treatments.
relevance_rationale: Mail-in rebate fulfillment is a fading industry; most functions now handled through instant digital coupons and retailer promotion platforms.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's 60/40/20 breakout anticipated the FTC's mid-2000s rebate enforcement push and the gradual big-box retreat from mail-in rebates by ~2015.
